skill.name: personal-tool-builder
skill.description: Guides users through building practical personal tools from real workflows, including CLI utilities, local-first apps, prototypes, and dogfooding practices.
skill.summary: Build focused personal tools from real problems, then refine them through daily use.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported high risk because Markdown code fences and instructional snippets matched execution and storage patterns. Review found no active executable files, no network behavior, no prompt injection attempt, and no evidence of malicious intent. Residual risk is low because users may copy local file or SQLite examples without adding validation and error handling.

low_findings.title: Markdown Fences Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings point to Markdown code fences and inline formatting in SKILL.md. They are documentation delimiters, not executable shell, Ruby, or process execution code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are visible Markdown delimiters or inline code markers. No actual shell command invocation or Ruby backtick execution is present.

low_findings.title: SQLite exec Example Misclassified as Python Exec
low_findings.description: The high severity exec finding is a better-sqlite3 schema creation example. It executes a static CREATE TABLE statement and does not evaluate user-controlled code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding lines show a SQLite database object and a static table definition. This is not Python exec and does not show dynamic code execution.

low_findings.title: Local File Storage Examples Require Care
low_findings.description: The skill includes examples using synchronous filesystem access in a home-directory data folder. This is legitimate for local-first tools, but copied code should add validation, permissions checks, and error handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem operations are real in the documentation snippet, but they are local examples rather than active skill behavior. Risk depends on how a user adapts the sample.

low_findings.title: Storage Technology Mentions Are Not Data Exfiltration
low_findings.description: The IndexedDB and SQLite findings are architectural recommendations for local-first applications. No evidence found of browser storage scraping, credential collection, network transfer, or hidden data movement.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are in a stack comparison and a local database setup example. There is no endpoint, network request, or instruction to collect sensitive browser data.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Matches Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: The blocker findings match ordinary words in the description and guidance sections. No evidence found of cryptographic code, weak hash algorithms, host reconnaissance commands, or privilege probing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are natural-language documentation or benign example fields. They do not contain cryptographic operations or system reconnaissance behavior.

content.user_title: Build Personal Tools That Solve Real Problems
content.value_statement: Many personal tools fail because they start from vague product ideas instead of daily friction. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code turn repeated personal workflows into focused tools.
actual_capabilities: Frames a personal pain point as a small tool with a clear first user.
Recommends CLI, script, desktop, or local-first app approaches for personal workflows.
Provides starter architecture patterns for Node.js and Python command-line tools.
Explains local storage options such as JSON files, SQLite, IndexedDB, and OPFS.
Identifies anti-patterns such as over-engineering and building for imaginary users.
Guides iterative dogfooding from a quick script toward a shareable product.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not include runnable project scaffolding files.
Example snippets need review before use in production or shared environments.
It focuses on personal tools more than enterprise collaboration platforms.
It does not provide complete security hardening for stored user data.

use_cases.title: Automate a Repeated Manual Task
use_cases.description: Turn a workflow you perform every day into a small script or CLI tool.

use_cases.title: Prototype a Local-First Productivity App
use_cases.description: Choose a local storage pattern and define the first version of an offline app.

use_cases.title: Refine an Internal Utility Through Dogfooding
use_cases.description: Use daily feedback to decide which rough edges deserve fixes before sharing.

prompt_templates.title: Find the First Tool Shape
prompt_templates.prompt: I repeat this task often: [describe task]. Help me decide the smallest personal tool that would save time this week.

prompt_templates.title: Design a CLI Utility
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a CLI tool for [workflow]. Include commands, inputs, outputs, storage needs, and a minimal first version.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Local-First App
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a local-first app for [problem]. Compare JSON files, SQLite, IndexedDB, and OPFS for this use case.

prompt_templates.title: Evolve a Personal Tool Into a Product
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my personal tool idea: [details]. Create a dogfooding plan, portability checklist, and sharing roadmap.

output_examples.input: I manually rename and sort downloaded invoices every Friday.
output: A narrow CLI tool that scans a download folder, previews proposed names, and moves approved files.
A first version that handles only your invoice format and logs every action for easy rollback.

output_examples.input: I want a personal notes app that works offline and stays private.
output: A local-first design using SQLite for structured notes and attachments stored in an app data directory.
A caution to add backups, import and export, and clear permission boundaries before relying on it daily.

output_examples.input: My script works for me, but I want to share it with teammates.
output: A portability checklist covering configuration, paths, error messages, setup instructions, and sample data.
A staged release plan that starts with two trusted users before a wider internal launch.

best_practices: Start with one real workflow and one user before adding broader features.
Use the tool daily and fix the friction you personally experience.
Replace hardcoded paths and assumptions before sharing the tool with others.
anti_patterns: Building for imagined users before proving that you need the tool yourself.
Adding complex architecture before a simple script has proved useful.
Skipping dogfooding and relying on abstract feature lists for direction.

faq.question: What kind of tools does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps plan personal scripts, CLI tools, local-first apps, and small automations based on repeated workflows.

faq.question: Does it generate a complete application?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance, patterns, and examples that you adapt into your own implementation.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude Code or Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is suitable for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.

faq.question: Is it only for developers?
faq.answer: It is most useful for technical users, but non-developers can use it to define clear tool requirements.

faq.question: Does it cover security?
faq.answer: It mentions security risks in personal tools, but copied examples still need a security review.

faq.question: When should I share a personal tool?
faq.answer: Share it after it solves your own problem reliably and you have removed personal assumptions from configuration.
